blurb: The big, goblet-flowered spring crocus — larger and a touch later than the snow crocus — that naturalizes through lawns in purple, white, and striped drifts to feed the very first bees.
water: Low; water in growth, dry summer dormancy
spread: Naturalizing; spreads by corm offsets and self-seeding into colonies, naturalizes well in lawns
seasons: Plump purple, white, or striped goblets late winter to early spring (Feb–Apr); grassy white-striped leaves through spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground summer–winter
wildlife: Early-spring pollen and nectar for queen bumblebees and emerging bees; flowers open in sun and close at night and in cloud
deer: Deer-resistant and largely rabbit-avoided, but squirrels, voles, and chipmunks dig and eat the corms — protect new plantings
toxic: Mildly toxic — corms contain colchicine-like alkaloids; ingestion may cause stomach upset in pets and people. CRITICAL LOOK-ALIKE: the DEADLY autumn crocus (Colchicum autumnale), which it closely resembles — spring Crocus has 3 stamens and blooms in spring with foliage present, while Colchicum has 6 stamens and blooms leafless in autumn. Never eat unmarked corms.

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3a–8b — very cold-hardy and dependable in Boulder's zone 5b–6a; established corms in well-drained soil need no mulch or winter protection.[1]
care.planting: Plant corms in fall, mid-September through late October, before the soil freezes, so they root over winter.[2] Set them pointed-end up about 4–5 in. (roughly 12 cm) deep and about 3–4 in. apart for these larger Dutch crocus corms.[3] Boulder's alkaline clay drains poorly — work coarse grit or sharp sand into the planting pocket and raise beds where drainage is sluggish, since wet winter or spring soil rots corms.[1] Pick a spot that bakes dry in summer (a sunny lawn, a south- or west-facing slope, under deciduous trees). In containers, use a fast-draining mix in a terracotta pot and overwinter it in an unheated garage or cold frame to buffer freeze-thaw heaving.[2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade — the more sun, the earlier and more prolific the bloom; flowers open in sunshine and close in cloud and at night, protecting the pollen. Under deciduous trees works well, with full sun while the corms flower in late winter and summer shade during dormancy.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained, gritty soil; sandy loam with some organic matter is ideal and it dislikes heavy, wet clay.[1] Neutral to alkaline is fine, so Boulder's pH suits it — amend clay planting pockets with grit and prioritize drainage, since waterlogged soil rots corms.[1][3]
care.water: Low. Water in at fall planting and again if fall is dry. During the short spring growing season, snowmelt and rain are usually enough; supplement only in a warm dry spell. Once foliage yellows in late spring, stop watering and let the soil bake dry through summer dormancy — summer moisture causes corm rot.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space corms about 3–4 in. apart for a natural drift; for an immediate mass, cluster many corms together (Dutch crocus are planted in large numbers — dozens per square foot — for lawn naturalizing) and let them multiply over the years.[1][3]
care.propagation: By offsets: lift clumps in summer dormancy, separate the offset cormlets, and replant immediately at depth — this is the main way they multiply. By seed: allow capsules to ripen, sow seed fresh in a cold frame or in a sheltered spot, and expect 3–4 years to first bloom; self-seeding into lawns and beds is a key naturalization mechanism, so leave some spent flowers to set seed if you want colonies.[1]
care.depth: Plant corms about 4–5 in. (≈12 cm) deep — slightly deeper than the small species crocuses, since Dutch corms are larger; a bit deeper still in very light sandy soil.[3]
care.bloom: Late winter into early spring on the Front Range, typically late February through April — a little larger-flowered and slightly later than snow crocus, extending the early-bee forage window.[1]
care.maintenance: Nearly hands-off once established. Let the foliage yellow and die back fully before removing it — the leaves recharge the corm for next year — so in lawns hold off mowing until the leaves brown in late spring.[3] Deadhead to limit self-seeding, or leave flowers to build colonies. Lift and divide overcrowded clumps every several years to keep flowering vigorous. Protect freshly planted corms from squirrels and voles with wire mesh laid over the bed for the first winter.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows where conditions suit, especially in undisturbed turf and open beds; seedlings take 3–4 years to bloom. In lawns, leave the grass uncut until the crocus foliage dies back so seed can ripen and the corms recharge — this is how Dutch crocus naturalize into sheets of bloom.[1][3]
care.troubles: Corm rot from poor drainage or summer irrigation is the main failure.[1] Squirrels, chipmunks, and voles dig and eat freshly planted corms — cover the planting area with wire mesh the first winter.[1] Birds occasionally peck the flowers. Improve airflow and avoid summer water to prevent rot and foliage moulds.[1]
care.companions: Naturalize through lawns and pair with other early bulbs — snow crocus, snowdrops, winter aconite, dwarf iris, and species tulips — and lean, sun-loving xeric plants that share its full-sun, sharp-drainage, dry-summer needs and bloom around the same time.[1]

edible.summary: Dutch crocus is not eaten — the corms contain alkaloids and are mildly toxic, and the critical hazard is confusion with the DEADLY autumn crocus (Colchicum), which looks nearly identical.[1][4]
edible.caution: The corms of Crocus vernus contain colchicine-like alkaloids that can cause stomach upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) if ingested; severity is generally low for a small accidental taste, but the plant is not a food crop and should not be eaten. The far greater danger is mistaking it for Colchicum autumnale (autumn crocus / meadow saffron), which is HIGHLY TOXIC and potentially fatal to people, dogs, cats, and horses: Colchicum contains colchicine, which causes cramping, bloody vomiting and diarrhea, respiratory failure, shock, multi-organ damage, and bone marrow suppression. Every part of Colchicum is dangerous — especially the bulb, which is nearly indistinguishable from a Crocus corm without a label.[4] How to tell them apart: true spring Crocus (including Dutch crocus) has 3 stamens and flowers in late winter/spring WITH foliage present; Colchicum has 6 stamens and flowers in autumn with NO foliage (its leaves emerge separately in spring). Never harvest unlabeled corms from the ground, and keep all Crocus/Colchicum corms and foliage away from children, dogs, cats, and livestock. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435) immediately.[1][4]
